top_rant.text:
This dress is indeed  worthy of a detailed if not plaintively emotional review. The reality is, if I wanted to wear a scratchy burlap sack, I could have gone to any of my local feed stores. After all, I do live in Oklahoma; and there is no lack of businesses providing  necessary goods for farmers and ranchers.
I see there are very positive reviews on this garment. I feel that speaks to the indomitable human spirit and the subjectivity of empirical experience. I'm sure that nearly everyone is able to have a distinct experience with this particular dress. However, in my case, it felt like the kind of polyester that you might have seen your third grade teacher wearing in perhaps the mid-70's. And of course I just dated myself there.
The texture reminded me of the extremely uncomfortable-looking dresses or slacks which harken back to be restrictive and misogynistic corsets of old. The rare original edition by Nathaniel Hawthorne, that moral narrative of the life of Hester Prynne, recounts that she was offered this dress in lieu of The Scarlet Letter to be emblazoned on her forehead; to which she curtly replied: "I'll take the letter thank you very much," while fishing a blazing A-shaped iron from the smoldering coals.
But this dress, right out of the bag, felt less like a garment and more like the kind of torture apparel commonly used by  nuns or priests in the 1600's to secretly atone for their sins and perform self penance in the privacy of their cloistered and candle-lit cells. 
Sliding it over my skin, I think it removed part of my epidermis. So really, it could be an exfoliating kind of garment; but that would require extreme rebranding. So I would say that, if one were to come home from a day in this dress, you will either need Neosporin, hydrocortisone, or a stiff drink to blot out the memory of the trauma, or perhaps an Epsom salt bath to soothe the countless micro wounds inflicted upon your skin from the duration of your wearing it.
It might be easy to surmise that I cannot recommend this garment, with one shameful exception: if you have the opportunity to gift it to a friend, who isn't really a friend; but who is really more of a " frenemy." Simply put,  they will thank you for it- because it's a reasonably attractive dress. But after wearing it for a day, they will know that you committed nothing less than passive aggressive dress-giving.  They will have no choice but to tell you that they received compliments on it; when in fact their entire body is now chafed, red, and is a testament to the kind of "friendship" that you and she truly share. Just be careful not to tell her where you got it, or you will surely receive the identical dress, in a different color for your next birthday, so that you guys can be  "twins." But do not be fooled.  It is really just a ploy for her to see you go through your day with the barely- concealed grimace of pain and stark betrayal on your face.
I would dub this be, "lets you really know where you stand with people," dress because if you wear it and you purposely brush up against a co-worker and they don't yelp in pain,  then you're in a unique position to know that- either they truly value you, or that they are an extremely duplicitous person who does not have your back. This is a person you should avoid at the water cooler and shy away from all invitations to enjoy salads at lunch, for this person will never tell you when you have spinach in your teeth or a knife in your back.
However, if somebody thoughtfully feels the dress and then tells you that they may now have to wear gloves to work, then I think this is someone with whom you can cultivate a deeper friendship. And in this way, this dress serves as a kind a social litmus test.  I think this could be very beneficial in the workplace, assuming that you have the fortitude to last the entire day in this torturous nightmare. Remember, in the immortal words of Frederick Nietzsche,  "when you look at the dress,  it looks back at you."
In conclusion, as a personal test of endurance and dissociation, or as a an instrument of speakable and divisive social detection vehicle, or as a means to gauge the true fortitude of your spirit in the face of unmitigated evil, I can recommend this dress. You may want to pair it with a yoke, or perhaps the stocks from an ancient public shaming area or even the kind of prong collar which has been abandoned by even the most fastidious of dog trainers, due to its inhumane means of control.
After a period of reflection in what I consider a far lesser instrument of corporeal testing, I meditated in the Iron Maiden in the corner of my room, because I find it far less constrictive and trendy- I feel I have undersold the stress and that there is another highly lucrative venue in which it will surpass all expectations.
The movie franchise of "Saw" is one which has endured many perplexing years and quite literally, every deviant conception of ways to test and torture people in the face of extreme duress surely of which one can conceive. One wonders how can be possible that screenwriters manage to manifest new and unspeakable terrors, pitting humanity against survival in a primal test to withstand these massications and regain not only once freedom but life itself. This is where this dress can truly shine; and I don't mean the sheen due to its potential radioactivity, as even the hellish brimstone dust that probably has left a kind of Dante-inspired 9th Circle patina on the garment, a telltale hallmark of the diabolical sweatshop in which it was surely forged.
I personally would gladly pay the full ticket price to see that weird little Muppet guy with the white mask ride out on his little tricycle with this monstrosity draped over the handlebars. It reminds one of William Blake's poem which is the counterpart to "Tyger", "The Lamb," in which he proposes that eternal cosmological query:  "Who made you dress? Dost thou know who made thee?"
We know and contrast that this dress is certainly not of the lamb but of the Beast, that rough Beast, with its "slow-moving thighs, slouching towards towards Bethlehem to be born."
The vivid imagery of this poem is palpable; but Blake would be posthumously gratified to learn that we now know why the Beast slouches; and we understand the source of the constriction and rash that his slow-moving thighs must elicit is because of the imprisoned by the unnecessarily cruel ruffle that crosses from the waist to the shoulder; and his thighs will be forever a testament to the universal potential for evil   symbolized by chafed thighs. Whether one is taking a quick jaunt to the local coffee shop or to that ancient city in an attempt to thwart the eventual Gospels or "Good News" which will soon follow, this is the dress to undermine all stories of trial and redemption. One might wonder if the Beast is trying to keep pace the three wise men who are probably dressed far more sensibly in robes which are both flowing and flattering for all body shapes and sizes. My money is on the Magi; because in this case, fashion and functionality play no part in the epic struggle to lay claim to the enduring nature of the human spirit; and if not learn from it, at least experience it as a morality play if you will, such as Faust.
Johann Wolfgang von Goethe had this dress in, I believe, the wine color. He wore this dress, much to the chagrin of the contemporaries, the writers and philosophers of his day, who all, in one dismissive gesture turned their backs on his wine-clad figure and said in unison with unbridled disdain: "I don't know her." It was from this crucible that the alchemical powers of the dress were truly realized. Therefore, I think that the dress is an instrument, not only of a nondescript shop in China (and may I say parenthetically- thank goodness I received it before the Trumpian tariffs really kicked in; because had I not been moved to purchase it when I did, it surely would have been marked up to a prohibitive $12.99 instead of the humble $9.00 that I paid). But we give unto Caesar what is Caesar's and while this caftan might suit Caesar, as it is somewhat reminiscent of a toga silhouette, he would have undoubtedly thrown himself down the Senate steps before he could have even  suffered that historic betrayal by those he trusted and considered both friends and supporters of his meteoric destiny. As Ovid and Herodotus both recounted for posterity that  "Thrice he tried the dress on and Thrice he refused it."
In summation, it really comes down to your intention. Do you wish to strive in certain futility like Sisyphus, ever rolling that rock up the hill- or adjusting his Spanx as a contemporary allegory, or do you seek to lash yourself to the mast so that the shrill tide of the Siren's song of ridicule and barely-restrained laughter in the wake of this dress conveying you into the depths, to be seen again.  Only you can answer this question. Only you can pay $9.00 and wait like the divine Siddartha under the Bodhi tree while you wait the three to six weeks it takes in order to complete the gauntlet that this dress provides.
God bless. Be strong, and for God's sake keep the receipt.

top_rant.text:
Automatic mechanical watches have never been my cup of tea, but this one kept popping up in online ads and catching my attention. At $100 for a Chinese fashion watch, my interest stopped at window shopping, but when I saw it on Amazon for a lot less than that, and then realized I could shave a few more bucks off the price with some reward points, I decided to give it a try.

The watch comes securely packed in a neat, black Lige box with a fold-over, magnetic lid, and small instruction booklet, cleaning cloth, warranty card, and a little tool you can use for pushing the two hidden buttons on opposite sides of the crown.

Features and Functions ...

The watch is equipped with hour/minute hands only (no second hand), and 3 sub-dials: day-of-week; calendar day, and 24-hour dial with underlying sun/moon/a.m./p.m. disc. The day-of-week and calendar day dials are adjusted by two hidden pushbuttons on opposite sides of the crown. One push = one unit on the corresponding dial. In normal operation, or when you’re adjusting the watch for the first time, don’t get thrown if both sub-dials don’t advance at midnight. The calendar-day sub-dial advances at midnight. The day-of-week subdial advances between 1:30 and 2:30 a.m.

The 24-hour dial revolves once every 24 hours. The sun/moon disc beneath the dial is NOT a moon phase indicator ... just an am/pm indicator. It’s literally mounted on the same post as the dial hand. The sub-dials and numbers/alpha characters on them are nicely designed, but really tiny, so if your vision isn’t so hot, you won't have much fun trying to see/use them.

The crystal isn’t flat, but a little convex, so it neatly compliments the rounded edges of the gold bezel surrounding it, and has a magnifying glass effect as you turn the watch sideways. The finish color on the body isn’t quite rose gold, and not quite yellow, but somewhere in between. I like it ... gives the watch an antique patina look. The main hands are the same color as the watch body. This watch is pretty “chunky” (I used a caliper to measure it: 15mm from crystal to back, 43mm in diameter). The diameter is fine ... the thickness, not so much. Even my divers watch is thinner.

It’s supposedly water resistant to 5 atmospheres (or, 50 meters/164 feet), but I wouldn’t trust this thing in water, at any depth or length of time. I’m guessing it’s far more vulnerable to water intrusion/damage than 5 atmospheres, e.g., at the crown opening. It’ll probably survive splashes and brief, shallow immersions, but get it out and dry it off promptly, and don’t press your luck.

In the face opening below the main hands, there’s a little adjustment lever and bar graph below it, marked from 0 to 20 in increments of 5. This lever isn’t accessible without opening the watch case ... probably there for use by service techs (or adventurous DIY’ers) to adjust the speed of the watch, if necessary. However, after 24 hours of careful comparison of the movement to the clock on my computer, the factory setting kept perfect time ... WHEN IT WAS RUNNING. More on that below.

The see-through back reveals a brass-colored auto-wind counterweight, which contrasts nicely with the stainless steel "guts" underneath it. The parts under the counterweight are all machined with attractive, linear patterns that greatly enhance the look of the see-through back.

About the band and clasp ...

The band that comes with the watch is supposedly leather ... dark brown, embossed with a croc pattern on the outside, and smooth gray leather on the inside. Honestly, I can’t tell if it’s genuine leather or not. If not, it’s a darn good fake ... sturdy, pliable ... a decent, attractive band for the price.

The band is fitted with a clasp that combines typical hole adjustment with the sort of folding clasp normally used on bracelet watches. Interesting, but I think I would have preferred a traditional buckle, mainly because I had a heck of a time figuring out how to work this thing. No explanation of how this particular clasp works in the instructions. It's actually pretty easy once you figure it out, though: select the hole/length that’s best for your wrist, and secure a separate clasp on that, then little spring-loaded buttons on opposite sides of the main clasp unlock/open up both ends of the band, more than wide enough to get the watch on/off your hand without having to mess with the hole clasp again. I guess that's the main benefit of this kind of clasp ... eliminates the wear/tear of taking a buckle on/off the leather every time, and greatly extends the service life of the band.

Best thing for me, I have a large-ish wrist (about 8 inches around), and the band/clasp actually fit me comfortably with the adjustment set on the second-to-last hole. The lug width of the band is 22mm (I measured it with a caliper, so trust me ... not the sales copy), so should be easy to find a replacement, if necessary.

About “automatic” mechanical watches ...

This stuff may seem obvious to all us old-timers, but for all you kids out there who’ve been weaned on battery-powered everything, mechanical watches don’t run on electricity from a battery. They’re powered by tension stored in a coiled mainspring. When the spring tension is fully released, the watch stops, and the spring has to be manually re-wound/tightened to restart the watch.

An automatic watch winds the mainspring via an internal counterweight that spins with normal movement of your arm/body while you’re wearing it. Thus, a properly functioning automatic watch should never have to be manually wound as long as you’re wearing it, and you’re reasonably active. However, that also means if you want the watch to run continuously without having to be reset or manually wound, you have to wear it pretty much every day. The implications for anyone who keeps/wears a number of different watches should be obvious ... you’re going to be re-starting/re-setting your automatic watch a lot.

A reminder that China is not Switzerland ...

Now, all the little gears and gizmos in a mechanical watch are pretty delicate, and all of them need to be made of the highest quality metals, and fit and work together very precisely for the watch to run normally and keep decent time. This is even more important in an automatic watch. All the problems traditionally associated with overwinding of the mainspring (e.g., breakage, movement getting stuck, etc.) are supposed to be controlled by a thing called a bridle, or, a kind of clutch that disables both auto and manual winding functions when the mainspring has reached maximum tension.

For some reason I never figured out, the watch I received kept stopping for no apparent reason, no matter how much I wound it, shook it to restart it, and/or tried to purposely spin the auto-winder. The problem is made worse by the fact that the manual winding mechanism on this watch is very quiet and low-resistance, e.g., hard/impossible to know when the mainspring is fully wound. In the end, this purchase reminded me why battery-powered quartz watches basically wiped out all makers of budget mechanical watches, and I ended up returning it to Amazon for a refund.

Conclusion ...

That’s the difference between a Chinese-made, automatic mechanical watch that costs less than a hundred bucks, and a German, Swiss or other, more-reputable equivalent that costs $500 (or more). The closer you look at the former, the less you like it. The closer you look at the latter, the more you like it, because you start to realize it cost what it did because the people who made it were life-long watchmakers who care more about their name/reputation, and the quality, function, reliability and longevity of the timepieces they make, than they care about making a quick buck off reverse-engineered, copy-cat junk, made from inferior materials that work just long enough to outlast the warranty (if you’re lucky), and are then forgotten and/or tossed in a landfill.

For the record, this is the first time I can remember ever giving a one-star rating to any product I've purchased on Amazon, and that's a LOT of products over the years ... very surprised and disappointed.

top_rant.text:
Well now!!! This one's something special, isn't it? A little nicer than the rest.
Of all the Casio's I own, this PRW-3500 is my favorite.
NOTE: This watch's LCD display is MUCH clearer and brighter than the Amazon image(s) show! See other web images, it's very impressive.
For comparison, I currently own:

PRG-270-1 (black, some orange print)
PRG-270D-7CR (titanium band)
PRW-3000-2BCR (blue/purple)
GW-9400-1CR Rangeman
WV-300 Wave Ceptor (discontinued)
And now, the PRW-3500-1CR

All of the new Casio sensor v3.0 watches have come a long way in accuracy. Where I couldn't accurately calibrate the earlier Pathfinders, I can now calibrate everything and get very accurate readings with the new Pro Trek series. Here are some comments on the functions:

COMPASS: Very accurate, works as good as my mechanical compasses. The movable bezel on this model is redundant, but still very useful and looks classy. Note: all magnetic compasses are negatively affected by magnetism, metal objects, etc. Best not to be used in the car, near electrical equipment or audio speakers which have powerful magnets. Best out in the field - and always calibrate right before using.

BAROMETER: Accurate to within .05 inHg. More accurate than than my iPhone Weather Channel App, because it's real-time, whereas with the phone app you have to wait for periodic updates plus there may be a discrepancy due to the Weather Channels' sensor location(s). The barometer's Weather Alert mode works great on all of my Pro Trek's. Activate it when you know foul weather is coming over the next 24 hrs, and it will alert you when storms are approaching. Also good for camping & hiking - you can activate it to be alerted about any sudden storm approaching so that you have enough time to find cover.

ALTIMETER: Affected by air pressure, so accuracy is more general, although the reading intervals have been improved. Best for hiking than car rides because snapshot intervals aren't suited for fast movement across terrain, they're suited for walking and cycling. Also, if you're hiking and a storm is approaching, your readings will not be accurate because changes to the barometric pressure will affect the altimeter readings. best use in stable weather. That said, it truly is the best technology available short of a GPS device.

TEMPERATURE: Very accurate. I have all of my Pro Trek's calibrated using a small electronic temperature gauge, and all of them read exactly the same as my wall-mounted digital temperature gauge (when located at the same location as the watches). You have to take the watch off to get accurate readings, and this will probably never change due to the proximity of the sensor to your body. The sensors are very sensitive - simply picking up the watch will effect the reading. I recommend keeping it off for 30 minutes (not 10, 15 or even 20) to give the watch enough time to fully acclimate. If temperate is important to you, clip the watch to your backpack or tent. Do not leave it in the sun.

BACKLIGHT: Works great. I love the brighter illumination of the new LED lights. In pitch dark you may be able to find items in your tent. I am very happy that Casio changed the afterglow limit from 1 second to 3 seconds. A one-second backlight limit (Pathfinder series) on any watch is an immediate deal breaker for me. I love the backlight's Auto-On feature: I wear one to bed and just flick my wrist to see the time whenever I wake in the middle of the night. I also love the location and knurled feel of the backight button - easy to feel in the dark.

BATTERY: I've been using a couple of these models for a while now and have yet to see a fully charged battery reach Medium from normal use. I suppose you can run it down pretty quickly if you keep using the backlight too much. When they're new I'll leave them on a sunny window sill all day for the first couple of days for maximum charge. After that I regularly put them in a window sill once or twice a week for several hours just to keep them topped them off.

ATOMIC TIMEKEEPING: Reception depends on your area and current weather conditions. This function only works at night, (usually 4am for me), even when wearing the watch. It doesn't work well for me on cloudy or bad weather nights, but you don't need to calibrate more often than once a week even if you're particular about accuracy. Don't expect to be able to calibrate 24 hrs a day unless you live very close to the radio transmission. I've got an Atomic Casio from 10 years ago and it has always synchronized, usually without me having to place it in a window. Keep it away from magnetic items like audio speakers.

ALARMS: The 5 alarms are cool to have, but there's no way to label them to distinguish which alarm is for what purpose. I love the Snooze alarm - however, I do wish it had the ability to choose specific days of the week, such as only M-F (exclude weekends). So it's either all or nothing with the alarms - you'll have to remember to turn them off when you don't need them to be on. The alarms on the Pro Trek's are about as loud as they get with Casio watches. Just so you know, if these aren't loud enough for you, don't bother looking at other digital watches. I also have the Casio G-Shock Rangeman (much more expensive than the Pro Treks) and its alarms aren't even as loud as the Pro Treks (that's not to discredit the Rangemen, it's an incredible timepiece!). So in any case, if you're going to wear your digital watch covered by the sleeve of your Gor-Tex down-lined wilderness coat in 20 MPH winds - it's not likely you're going to hear the alarms. And I doubt you'll hear them in your nylon tent flapping in the winds at the top of that mountain peak either. It's just not gonna happen. In fairness, I know of no other digital watches that are louder. And for those who think a vibration feature is the answer, imagine the cost, size and need for more battery power being a major obstacle.

SUNRISE/SUNSET: Once I properly calibrated for the LAT/LONG of my exact location, this feature is now in perfect sync with The Weather Channel app on my iPhone. (You'll have to find the lat/long website calculator for your area, Amazon won't allow me to post the link.)

Other functions such as the stopwatch, countdown timer, world time, hourly chime, etc. are all standard features, and work as well as you would expect. v3.0 ProTrek watches are as accurate as the technology gets without GPS, but here's the best thing: they are the simplest effective gadgets to use! No batteries to change, no need to ever set the time and date again, and the user-interface is intuitive (after a quick read through the booklet). And the learning curve, if there even is one, is low and even fun. The instructions are great, and provide lots of important tips for best use.

ONLY ONE CON: The band is very difficult to remove on my PRG-270-1, and this one is even more difficult due to the hole in the rubber strap retainer. It's very frustrating trying to get the strap to slide out from under that retainer. The best way I've found so far is you have to be able to use one hand to both pull the strap out while also squeezing the rubber retainer enough to get the strap's hump out from under it. This would make more sense if you own the watch and can see it visually. But this is by no means a deal breaker, as long as I can get the watch off without breaking it, I'm OK.

Highly recommended!!
I'm very excited to own this beautifully made electronic timepiece.
Casio has come a long way.
Don't hesitate to buy one!
top_rant.title: This One's Special!

top_rant.text:
(SCROLL DOWN FOR PROS AND CONS LIST. First paragraphs are my impression and description.)

I really love this set of nose rings. I usually wear stud style nose rings but every now and then i like to change it up. Hoop nose rings, in my opinion, can really change up your look and style and can noticeably add to an outfit. They are a more noticeable, eye catching style, which will be noticed easier than small studs that tend to be more subtle. The open hoop style is one I prefer and I find them easier to wear. It leaves some open space in your nostril that I find far more comfortable for day to day wear. (TMI warning! LoL – They are WAY easier to deal with when you need to blow your nose! When I have a full hoop in my nose it can really hurt trying to blow my nose and having to maneuver it.)

This set of nose rings came in a handy little plastic box that can hold them safely for travel or storage. They were each individually wrapped in plastic sleeves before I opened them. There are five colors provided: silver, yellow/gold, black, a blue chrome finish, and a multi-colored chrome finish that reflects different colors in different light (it leans predominantly more green/yellow and resembles an ‘oil slick’ appearance). The color selection includes all the ‘basic’ colors for a wardrobe I could want. They are core colors that will match most of my jewelry and other accessories that I would add to a look. On top of having the go to colors of black, silver, and gold – they also include two REALLY fun ones for more funky moods and I LOVE the chrome finish on the blue nose ring as well as the REALLY COOL effect the ‘oil-slick’ one has.

PROS
-Great price for a set of 5 nose rings. (~$2 for a nose ring is a great deal - Especially for a hoop in my buying experience!)
-Comes with a durable handy plastic case for storage.
-GREAT QUALITY for the PRICE! (I have worn the silver one for most of the month I have had these and it is still in GREAT condition.)
-Sellers pictures accurately show how the rings will look.
-SHINY clean finishes that have not dulled or discolored with time. (I have had these about a month and they still look new and as they were when I got them.)
-GREAT VERSATILE color selection! (Includes all basic colors for most wardrobes/matching accessories.)
-UNIQUE , VERY COOL, multi-chrome finish on one! (It really looks like an ‘oil slick’ and I love the effect. It reflects different colors when caught in different light!)
-Popular 20 gauge size that will fit most nose piercings. (My nose was irritated when I first got it in, but I adjusted and they were very comfortable to wear and fit well.)
-Stainless Steel made and does not irritate my skin! (It was only irritated when I first put it in because I had tons of trouble getting it in there!)
-It does not fall out or shift much. It stays put once it is in.
-OPEN HOOP style that allows space inside the nostril and is more comfortable to wear than closed hoops that must be clicked shut. (Blowing your nose is so much easier with this style rather than closed hoops!)
-When put in the nose the right way for the odd design of the hoop, the gap DOES NOT show and it looks like a closed hoop in the nose.
-Appropriate size hoop that sits well in my nostril. (It doesn’t look ‘too big’ or ‘too small’ for my nose and it fits well. I provided a picture showing it in for an idea on the size.)

CONS
- POOR DESIGN. They have to be put it from the INSIDE of the nostril going OUT! They are NOT EASY to get in! (I have never had to put a nose ring in from the INSIDE of the nostril going OUT so it was quite the ordeal getting the hoop in at first. I could NOT find the piercing on the inside of my nostril and irritated my piercing trying to get it in and there was some slight swelling afterwards. I can’t complain for $2 a ring though and am still happy with this purchase – it is a CHALLENGE to get these in though so fair warning!!!)

Overall, I am REALLY happy with this purchase. You really can’t beat the price for what you get. The rings look to be high quality as well as feeling durable and sturdy. I have had these about a month and they still look brand new and have worn VERY WELL. The size of the hoop fits my face and is a good size. The set includes all the ‘basic’ colors you could want and I LOVE that black is included! It is great having gold and silver for when you want to wear different jewelry but the black is my favorite because I have A LOT of black in my wardrobe and it goes with EVERYTHING! The blue and multi-chrome ones were just added bonuses for me and really great colors. I have blue eyes so I love that blue was part of the set. (Blue is a REALLY popular color anyway it seems, so I think it was a great choice.) They feel comfortable in and I don’t even feel it there that I forget I have a hoop in sometimes. The gap can’t be seen when it is put in but it allows more space in the nostril. The only complaint is the odd design. I didn’t know there were nose rings that had to be put in from the INSIDE OF THE NOSE! LoL In order for the gap to be hidden in the nose and for the ‘stopper’ or circle on the end of the hoop to be hidden, it has to be inside the nose. I just think it is so backwards. It was CRAZY HARD to get these suckers in at first and there were SEVERAL ATTEMPTS before I got them in. If you are up for the challenge and want to save money then they are WORTH it. If you don’t want to deal with getting them in and performing what seems like some pseudo-surgery looking in a mirror up your nose feeling around like you’re blind dodging nose hair, then DON’T GET THESE! If you are one to change your nose rings A LOT, and want these hoops to specifically wear for SHORT TERM periods, I would say the hassle of getting them in IS NOT WORTH IT. Once I finally got mine in, my piercing was slightly swollen, and to go through all that just to try and put a different color hoop in the next day, it wouldn’t be worth it to me. IF I was just putting the hoop in for a day and then switching to a stud I would be fine with it though! I received these at a discount for my honest and unbiased review. These opinions are my own and I was not swayed in any way. If you have any questions let me know and I will do my best to help!
top_rant.title: Must be put in from the INSIDE of your nostril going OUT for the gap to be hidden!

top_rant.text:
These are good pearl earrings for the price. The pearls are the size promised. They aren't quite perfectly round, they don't quite match, and they have a bit less luster than pricier pearls would. That said, the posts are hypoallergenic (I am very allergic to pot metals), and I can relax wearing them knowing that if I loose an earring, I haven't lost the money. This brand has all different sizes of pearls with the sterling silver coated in rhodium posts at reasonable price points. Given the price of pearls, this can be a good way to try some different sizes out, before getting fancier pearls in a gold setting.

Are they real?: First off, Jaqueline Kennedy's famous pearls were fake. Good quality fakes are pricier than lower grades of pearls. If you don't believe me, then look for a gem show near you, pay the entry fee, and see what you can get for what price with pearls by the strand. You will be surprised. Meanwhile, I have some very gorgeous 1950s vintage fake pearls that I got new old stock that are much nicer than any real pearls in my price range. There's a huge difference between a high quality fake and some garbage plastic fake. Jaqueline Kennedy's fake pearls were valued at $400-$500 by Sotherby's in the mid-1990s, to give an idea of how good fakes cost more than lower end real pearls. Now that that is out of the way, these Abbie+Cleo pearls are real. I touched my teeth to them, and they have the feel of pearl rather than of painted glass. But it also doesn't matter. Unless you are spendy to an extreme degree, fake and real overlap in price and quality, and ultimately what you see is what you get. Appearance is all that matters in this price range.

Pearl quality: These are semi round, rather than perfectly round. My two pearls don't perfectly match, and the shape being slightly different is the difference. Color and luster matches. That said, when I'm wearing them, I think someone would have to be all up on me darting from side to side and comparing to notice the small difference in shape. They also have lower luster than would more expensive pearls. I think these earrings are totally fine. They are about what you would expect for the price. The pearls are pretty nice but not perfect. All those flaws hit the price point, but they are still pretty and good enough to look fine. I think it's a fair deal for the price.

Hypoallergenic: I am very allergic to metal. I generally have to have a high carat gold or name brand surgical steel for earrings, or else my ear holes get inflamed and weep and it gets bad fast. I've been wearing these Abbie+Cleo earrings for about two days during day time, and no allergic reaction. They are good.

Posts and setting: These are on sterling silver posts coated in rhodium. Rhodium is hypoallergenic, and sterling silver is potentially a good hypoallergenic option (although really it has to be sterling to where the 7.5 percent that isn't silver is a hypoallergenic metal too). I took the chance on these, because with the rhodium plating, I thought the way they are made is focused on preventing allergies, and I think I lucked out and got what I wanted. Now... the other thing going on with sterling silver as a setting for pearls is that it's possibly going to cause complications with cleaning. I assume that the rhodium plating will eventually wear off. Then the silver will tarnish. Meanwhile, pearls are picky about washing, and I have to clean my earrings at least every other wear or the dead skin will get gross and I can get an infection. You have to wash pearls in soapy water with mild soap, then dry them with a soft cloth. No hydrogen peroxide soak, no alcohol soak, about no other method of cleaning will work on pearls. And water tarnishes silver faster, and silver polish is not good near pearls. Basically, the sterling silver setting is eventually going to be nearly black from tarnish and there's nothing to do about it. On these earrings, the posts and backs hardly show, so almost no one will notice it, but just be aware.

This company makes these pearl earrings with these rhodium and sterling silver settings in a wide variety of sizes. I think that what they are doing is putting the slightly lower luster pearls in the silver settings, and the higher luster pearls in the gold settings, and pricing accordingly. Since real pearls can be pricey, these comparatively lower priced pearls in the silver setting may be a good way to see what size best suits your face. That's what I did - I got this big 11-12mm size, and also the 7-8mm size in these low cost pearls, in order to see what size I like best. And, by the way, you might think that bigger would be better, but actually for my face, the 7-8mm size which is much smaller was a better look. Happily with the lower priced Abbie+Cleo array of sizes, I found out, and now I know for sure, so if I were to blow money on higher grade pearls in a gold setting, I know what is best for me and will really suit me.

Are these worth it?: Having gone to all kinds of gem show with my mom at various times, yes, I think that these are worth it. The pearls are matched well enough, especially for such a big size of pearl. (For the smaller 7-8mm pearls I got from them, the two pearls were almost an exact match. The smaller ones cost about 2/3 as much, I think maybe the big pearls are a little less regularly shaped to hit that comparable price point.) Where they loose out is: not being perfectly round, not perfectly matching one another, and lower luster. Where they win big time is: real and the size and look promised from the online description, and hypoallergenic setting which is fairly hard to find when buying online. These really do look nice enough. I'm sure that for three thousand dollars, I could get perfect round, matched, big, high luster pearls. And, I'm sure that for three hundred dollars I could get a small step up. For the price, at thirty dollars these 11-12mm ones are totally a win. It's just, you have to pay more to get more. Even good fakes would be cost more, and with almost any fake, you have to worry about the posts having pot metal in them. Really, these look OK, and with pearls, and maybe any jewelry, looks is what matters.

Overall, these are a decent set of earrings and an OK buy. The two main reasons to get these are: Try out a big size at a low price point, and see if the big size suits your face and tastes. And, get pearl earrings that look OK, but are not expensive to where it's a huge deal if one falls off and gets lost. I personally, do not like expensive earrings for this stress of having to worry whether one might fall out. It almost never happens, but if it happened with something expensive, I would be sad and I would have to go looking for the lost earring. These are low priced enough, I wouldn't be so sad or take the time to look and look. Or for girls, this is a low stress option, since they can have pearls that are real and are nice enough, but if they loose one, then it's not a huge loss. Also, they are good for gifting. The hypoallergenic post was hypoallergenic for me, and I have sensitive ear holes, and that's important with a gift, where you don't know if the recipient has allergies.
top_rant.title: Hypoallergenic posts, for real; pearls are real and are good enough to look fine

top_exclaim.text:
I REALLY WANTED TO LOVE THIS, BUT....so much is disappointing. First of all the fit. I am a very tiny lady; 5' 2" and weigh 90 lbs. I knew this was 100 % cotton and I wash my clothes so I knew it would shrink. I ordered a medium to be sure it was roomy enough after shrinkage. I tried it on right out of the bag and could barely get my head through the neck opening, which only has a single " button" at the neck. The other buttons on the front placket are fake- don't open or close, so just decoration! It was very long, which I anticipated and knew I would have to take up. Since I normally wear a size 2, this should have been more than roomy enough for me to get into!!! NOT!
I washed it as I always do and the first major issue was a good 10 inches of the beautiful decorative metalic thread on the hem disintigrated and unravelled!!! Destroyed! A very noticable gap!!!
It is a very good thing that I am a professional seamstress and can sew anything! I knew before I washed it that I would have to take apart the front placket and make all the buttons operable and that would take a few hours to re-make the placket, which is only decorative and not fully made! Since I knew too, that I would have to shorten it, I knew I would have that left-over fabric to create the placket!
I will take the length up by taking apart the seam that connects the skirt part to the bodice, and that will look very nice when done! This will keep the pretty fancy stitching at the hem intact. BUT, I will have to take a significant amount of time to recreate, duplicate and apply the fancy metalic thread trim embroidery at the hem that was destroyed!!! NOT happy about that!!!
Plus, that single button at the top? It doesn't have a button hole, it has a cheap hook and loop to close it! NOT nice at all!
For $50, this should have been a lot nicer than this....[ esp.. since it's made in India!..] ONLY because I do love the fabric itself and the design and can rework the whole thing, am I keeping it! Otherwise, I would send it back!
BUYERS beware!!!
UPDATE: Aug 22.22. I decided to return this given how cheap it is and the huge repairs needed. Seller did not respond to my request. Seller did not respond to issuing return ship label! I ended up calling Amazon twice to get this fixed. Seller did not respond again! Second call to Amazon asking for the A-Z guarantee got me the refund. Now, over a week late, seller wants me to pay for sending this defective item back to India from USA! Ain't happening!
DO NOT buy from this seller!!! In fact, be very wary of buying things that look like they might be shipped from third party and from India! Not worth the trouble and disappointment!
Thank you Amazon for getting my $50 refund!
top_exclaim.title: I REALLY WANTED TO LOVE THIS SO MUCH, BUT........

[Note: this is for the blue and gold colorway]. These are a pretty convincing copy of Dior's Technologic sunnies. When it comes to ordering sunglasses online it is a crap shoot every time even when specks are included and in this case, they thankfully turned out to be the perfect, slightly over sized aviator with beautiful deep blue lenses I was looking for. This particular color seems to be a big trend this year and its great to buy these instead of spending on a trend that may only last a few minutes.

It does seem that based on what color you purchase, the sizing might be different. On some people these can look huge and on others normal.Mine in this blue color are about 6 inches at the widest top crossbar area. The lenses themselves are about 2 and a quarter long. I did notice that men seem to prefer this style over the So Real frames also by Dior as these come out wider at the temples. These glasses have a nice weight to them but the arms feel very loosely screwed. It concerns me they might break/fall off. These also came with a black dust bag. Overall mine came exactly as pictured and are holding up well with a quality slightly better than F21 or H and M.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I love this hoodie! I was worried it would be that crappy cheap, thin, slick material you get with some of these cheaper printed hoodies, but it's actually very nice material, and quite a bit warmer than I expected it to be. The fleece inside is soft, and it's super cozy. The print isn't super high quality, but it still looks great. Overall it feels very well made with rather sturdy stitching, and is honestly a steal for the price. I ordered another similarly priced hoodie along with this one that looked to be a similar material, and that one turned out to be complete garbage.

This hoodie also runs quite small. I'm normally a solid small in most clothes, but I sized up to L/XL and it fits me perfectly. Readings reviews to see how things fit (especially if they're sized according to normal Asian sizes!) is important.
top_five_star_obscene.title: Thin, but surprisingly warm and comfy, and runs SMALL. If you normally wear small, order L/XL

Fit as expected. Forget Hanes, FTL is the way to go as you will get far less riding up on the leg, less wedgie, less curled waistbands and less hanging open fly for dick-on zipper hell. I with the material was a little more substantial for duality but I also like not having swamp balls from wrapping them in a thick blanket of cloth on hot days.

Also no scratchy tags! These don't last forever, but then nothing does, and least they are price where I can affort to replace all my pairs annually.
